text.title: State, Chain of Responsibility, Template Method, and Mediator
text.summary: When-to/when-NOT rules, modern trends, and selection triggers for State (FSM), Chain of Responsibility (middleware pipelines), Template Method (algorithm skeletons), and Mediator (centralized component communication).

rule.statement: Apply State when an object's behavior changes significantly across multiple states AND the state-transition logic is growing into a complex conditional. With 2–3 states and simple transitions, an enum + switch is cleaner.
rule.rationale: State pattern promotes each state to a class; this is only worth the overhead when per-state behavior has real complexity. Simple enums with minimal branching don't justify the class explosion.

list.item: Use when: object behavior varies significantly by internal state, complex conditional logic based on state, implementing finite state machines (order lifecycle, document workflow).
Avoid when: only 2–3 simple states with minimal logic, states rarely change.
State vs Strategy: both change behavior at runtime, but State transitions automatically based on internal events; Strategy is set externally by the client.
Modern TypeScript: XState provides typed state machines with guards, actions, and hierarchical states. Discriminated unions give type-safe FSM representation without a library.
Functional state machines: pure transition functions (currentState, event) → nextState with no side effects in the transition are easier to test and reason about.

rule.statement: Use Chain of Responsibility for middleware pipelines where each handler decides to process the request or pass it on. Prefer a function-composition approach (list of handler functions iterated in order) over class-based chains for simple pipelines.
rule.rationale: Class-based chains add boilerplate for simple middleware cases; a list of handler functions achieves the same decoupling with less ceremony in languages with first-class functions.

list.item: Use when: multiple handlers may process a request, handler set should be configurable at runtime, implementing middleware pipelines (auth, validation, logging, rate limiting).
Avoid when: only one handler needed, request must always be handled (use a default/fallback handler instead), order is fixed and handlers never change.
Fluent set_next: returning the next handler from set_next enables builder-style chain construction: handlerA.set_next(handlerB).set_next(handlerC).

Modern trends: Express/Koa/Hono middleware patterns, pipeline operators in functional languages, functional composition chains. ASGI middleware in FastAPI/Starlette is Chain of Responsibility implemented with callables.

rule.statement: Apply Template Method when multiple classes share the same algorithm structure but differ in steps. If algorithm structure itself varies, use Strategy instead.
rule.rationale: Template Method uses inheritance to fix the skeleton; Strategy uses composition to swap the whole algorithm. Inheritance is harder to test and refactor; prefer Strategy when both could work.

list.item: Use when: algorithm structure is fixed and multiple subclasses share the workflow, framework extension points needed (lifecycle hooks), data processing pipelines with variant steps.
Avoid when: algorithm needs complete flexibility (use Strategy), only one implementation exists (YAGNI — no base class needed).
Hooks: optional steps with a default implementation (no-op or sensible default) that subclasses can override without being forced to implement. Distinguished from abstract steps by having a body.

Modern trends: hooks and lifecycle methods in React (useEffect, componentDidMount) are Template Method at the framework level. Protocol/trait-based approaches replace inheritance in Rust and modern Go.

rule.statement: Use Mediator to reduce many-to-many coupling between components. Stop when the Mediator becomes a god object that knows too much — split into multiple mediators by domain.
rule.rationale: A Mediator that coordinates all components becomes a single point of failure and a maintainability bottleneck. The pattern solves coupling; one Mediator per domain keeps it bounded.

list.item: Use when: many components communicate in complex ways, reducing coupling between N components to 1 (each knows only the mediator), centralized control point needed.
Avoid when: simple direct communication suffices, Mediator would accumulate too many responsibilities (god object).

Mediator vs Observer: Observer is distributed (subject notifies subscribers directly); Mediator is centralized (all communication routes through one object). Mediator gives more control; Observer gives more decoupling.

Modern trends: message brokers (RabbitMQ, Kafka, Redis Pub/Sub) are Mediator at the infrastructure level. Redux/Vuex are UI Mediators. Event bus implementations (MediatR in .NET, EventBus in Android) apply the pattern within a process.
